claim: Highly spherical Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder with a narrow particle-size distribution, low oxygen content, and good flowability was prepared using the plasma rotating electrode process (PREP).
evidence_text: Highly spherical Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni powder with a narrow particle-size distribution, low oxygen content, and good flowability was prepared using the plasma rotating electrode process (PREP).

claim: Sc microalloying of René 104 superalloy powder reduced average particle size from 38.3 μm to 29.6 μm, improved flowability from 43.5 s to 15.8 s, decreased O and S contents, increased relative density of as-printed alloy to 99.4%, reduced grain size by 46.8%, increased nano-hardness by 13.9%, and eliminated cracks.
evidence_text: The results demonstrated that the powder properties of Sc-microalloyed René 104 superalloy were conspicuously enhanced with the decreased contents of O and S elements, the reduced average particle size from 38.3 μm to 29.6 μm, the decreased flowability from 43.5 s to 15.8 s. Thus, the relative density of as-printed René 104 alloy increased to 99.4%, the grain size decreased by 46.8%, the nano-hardness increased by 13.9%, and the cracks were completely eliminated.

claim: The PREP-prepared Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder is highly spherical with no satellites or irregular particles, as shown in Fig. 2(a) and (b).
evidence_text: The PREP-prepared Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder is highly spherical with no satellites or irregular particles observed, as shown in Fig. 2(a) and (b).

claim: EDS mapping reveals that Fe, Mn, Al, and Ni are uniformly distributed in the powder particles.
evidence_text: The Fe, Mn, Al, and Ni in the Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder are clearly uniformly distributed (Fig. 2(c)-(f)).

claim: The particle size mainly ranges from 15 to 53 μm with a median particle size (D50) of 35.69 μm.
evidence_text: Particles with sizes mainly distributed in the 15-53 μm range are observed in Fig. 3; the median particle size (D50) was determined to be 35.69 μm.

claim: XRD of the PREP-prepared powder shows diffraction peaks of both γ-Fe(M) and α-Fe(M) phases; the γ-Fe(M) phase dominates because slow cooling allows carbon dissolved in α-Fe to promote ferrite formation.
evidence_text: The XRD pattern of the Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder is shown in Fig. 4, which shows diffraction peaks for both the γ-Fe(M) and α-Fe(M) phases. The γ-Fe(M) phase dominates because the PREP-prepared Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder was cooled relatively slowly, resulting in a small amount of carbon dissolved in the α-Fe phase that promotes the formation of the ferrite phase.

claim: In contrast to the powder, the Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni alloy SLM-printed at each laser power mainly comprises the austenite phase without any α-Fe(M) phase, which helps increase strength and toughness.
evidence_text: In contrast to the Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder, the Fe-30Mn-9Al-C3-Ni alloy SLM-printed at each laser power mainly comprises the austenite phase without any α-Fe(M) phase (Fig. 5). This helps to increase the strength and toughness of low-density steel.

claim: Relative density first increases then decreases with laser power: P-70 98.27%, P-90 99.16%, P-110 99.34%, P-130 99.12%, P-150 99.07%.
evidence_text: P-70, P-90, P-110, P-130, and P-150 exhibited relative densities of 98.27%, 99.16%, 99.34%, 99.12%, and 99.07%, respectively

claim: The minimum density among the SLM-printed samples is 6.78 g/cm³, which is much lighter than 304 stainless steel (7.93 g/cm³).
evidence_text: the minimum density is 6.78 g/cm³, which is much lighter than the density of 304 stainless steel, 7.93 g/cm³.

claim: To minimize laser vector coincidence and reduce crystallographic texture, a 67° rotation angle was applied to each printed layer.
evidence_text: To minimize the laser vector coincidence of adjacent layers and reduce the crystallographic texture, a 67° rotation angle was applied to each layer [24].

claim: SLM printing used laser powers of 70, 90, 110, 130, 150 W (corresponding energy densities 58.33–125.00 J/mm³) with fixed scanning speed 750 mm/s, spot diameter 0.1 mm, layer thickness 0.02 mm, hatch spacing 0.08 mm, substrate preheating at 200 °C, and argon atmosphere with O₂ <300 ppm.
evidence_text: The substrate was heated to 200 °C prior to printing. During printing, argon (99.99% pure) was used to protect each sample from oxidation, and the oxygen content in the forming chamber was maintained below 300 ppm. Laser powers of 70, 90, 110, 130, and 150 W were used, which correspond to laser energy densities (E) of 58.33, 75.00, 91.67, 108.33, and 125.00 J/mm³, respectively. The spot diameter, layer thickness, and hatch space were 0.1, 0.02, and 0.08 mm, respectively. The scanning speed was 750 mm/s.

claim: EBSD inverse pole figure maps of cross-sections reveal mainly fine equiaxed grains with random orientations due to the 67° per-layer rotation, while the longitudinal section of P-110 is dominated by columnar grains extending across deposition layer boundaries, with a small amount of equiaxed grains at melt pool bottoms.
evidence_text: Fig. 7(a)-(e) present inverse pole figures (IPFs) that correspond to the electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) maps of Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni alloy cross-sections, which mainly reveal fine equiaxed grains with random orientations that are represented by different colors. This result is attributable to the use of a rotation angle of 67° for each printing layer during the SLM process. On the other hand, columnar grains dominate the vertical section of the P-110 sample. A small amount of equiaxed grains that are primarily distributed at the bottom of the melt pool are also observed (Fig. 7(f)).

claim: Average grain sizes in cross-sections increase with laser power: P-70 8.1 μm, P-90 10.6 μm, P-110 11.0 μm, P-130 13.9 μm, P-150 14.3 μm. The longitudinal section of P-110 has a larger average grain size of 14.9 μm.
evidence_text: average grain sizes of 8.1, 10.6, 11.0, 13.9, and 14.3 μm determined for the P-70, P-90, P-110, P-130, and P-150 samples, respectively (Fig. 8(a-e)). Moreover, the longitudinal section of the P-110 sample exhibited an average grain size of 14.9 μm, which is larger than the cross-sectional value of 11.0 μm (Fig. 8(f)).

claim: No ferrite diffraction patterns are detected in the SLM-printed samples, indicating that fast cooling during SLM suppresses ferrite formation.
evidence_text: ferrite diffraction patterns were not detected because ferrite formation in each Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni alloy was suppressed by fast cooling during the SLM process.

claim: SEM images show that the P-110 sample has the best surface quality without obvious unfused powder particles or pores, whereas the P-70 sample contains numerous unfused particles, and small spheres form at 130-150 W due to localized heat accumulation.
evidence_text: Numerous unfused powder particles and pores were observed on the surface at a laser power of 70 W (Fig. 11(a)); these unfused powder particles and pores gradually decreased in number as the laser power was increased. The P-110 sample, prepared at a laser power of 110 W, exhibited the best surface quality; it was devoid of any obvious unfused powder particles or pores (Fig. 11(c)) because a higher laser power increases the size of the melt pool, which benefits the metal-fusion process and improves surface quality. However, small spheres were increasingly generated as the laser power was increased to 130 and 150 W owing to localized heat accumulation (Fig. 11(d) and (e)).

claim: Each SLM-printed Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni sample contains austenite as the predominant phase with a small amount of nano-sized κ carbide.
evidence_text: Each SLM-printed sample contained austenite as the predominant phase with a small amount of nano-sized κ carbide.

claim: The as-cast Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni alloy has a yield strength of 772.3 MPa, tensile strength of 879.0 MPa, reduction of area 17.10%, and elongation of 20.53%, which are inferior to the SLM specimens.
The P-110 SLM sample exhibited yield strength of 887.1 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 1076.8 MPa, reduction of area of 35.16%, and total elongation of 40.80% (Table 2).
The as-cast sample showed yield strength 772.3 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 879.0 MPa, reduction of area 17.10%, and elongation 20.53%, all lower than those of the SLM-printed samples (Table 2).
The fracture surface of P-110 was dominated by dimples, indicating a ductile fracture mode (Fig. 13c).
evidence_text: Dimple 20 µm
